ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code exists for dal khichdi; A0CKJ (rice dish) used as closest parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for dal khichdi or khichdi as a specific food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of dal khichdi is approximately 300 g, equivalent to one medium bowl as typically served in a North Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Khichdi (rice and lentil preparation) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN reports values for khichdi (rice and moong dal preparation). Values adapted for a standard dal khichdi recipe using basmati rice and yellow moong dal with ghee. Per-100g values are consistent with ICMR-NIN khichdi entry and corroborated by USDA SR Legacy values for constituent ingredients (cooked rice: ~130 kcal/100g; cooked moong dal: ~105 kcal/100g). Composite dish estimate weighted at approximately 60% rice, 35% lentils, 5% ghee/spices.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for dal khichdi in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 51 is estimated based on the closest analogous entry: mixed rice and lentil dish (khichdi) referenced in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The lentil component lowers the overall GI relative to plain white rice (GI ~72). A GI of 51 (low category) is consistent with peer-reviewed evidence for rice-lentil combinations. GL per serving calculated as: (51 × 19.5 × 300) / (100 × 100) = 29.9, rounded to 29.5 per 300 g serving.

hypertension.guidance: Dal khichdi contains approximately 210 mg sodium per 100 g (630 mg per 300 g serving), which represents a moderate sodium load; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to manage daily sodium intake within the recommended 1500–2000 mg/day threshold.

type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal khichdi has a low glycemic index of approximately 51 due to the lentil content, but the glycemic load per standard 300 g serving is approximately 29.5 g, which is high; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dal khichdi is relatively low in saturated fat (approximately 1.4 g per 100 g) and provides dietary fibre from lentils (approximately 1.2 g per 100 g), making it a heart-friendly staple when prepared with minimal ghee; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with reduced ghee to further lower saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Khichdi is a traditional North Indian one-pot comfort dish made by slow-cooking yellow lentils (moong dal or toor dal) together with basmati rice, ghee, cumin seeds, turmeric, and salt until the mixture reaches a soft, porridge-like consistency. The dish is deeply rooted in Indian culinary tradition and is considered one of the most easily digestible and nourishing preparations in Ayurvedic and everyday Indian cooking. The texture is soft and slightly mushy, with a mild, earthy flavour from the lentils balanced by the aromatic warmth of cumin and turmeric. Ghee adds a subtle richness and a characteristic nutty aroma. Nutritionally, dal khichdi provides a complementary amino acid profile from the rice-lentil combination, moderate protein (approximately 4.2 g per 100 g), complex carbohydrates (approximately 19.5 g per 100 g), and dietary fibre from the lentils. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 51 (low category), making it a more glycaemically favourable option than plain white rice. It is naturally gluten-free and suitable for vegetarians. In Singapore, dal khichdi is served in Indian restaurants and tiffin establishments, often as a comfort meal or as part of a thali. Clinically, it is a moderate-sodium dish and the glycemic load per standard serving is high enough to warrant portion control for individuals with type 2 diabetes or hypertension.
common_user_queries: How many calories in dal khichdi?
Is dal khichdi good for diabetes?
What lentils are used in khichdi?
Is khichdi good for weight loss?
Is dal khichdi high in sodium?
What is the GI of khichdi?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for 'Sadhuwain Ki Dal Khichdi' as a named restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN khichdi (rice and moong dal) data and corroborated by USDA SR Legacy ingredient-level data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for a rice-lentil mixed dish. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. The input food_group was 'Meat and alternatives', which is incorrect for a rice-lentil dish; corrected to 'Grains and staples'. 2. The restaurant name 'Sadhuwain Ki Dal Khichdi' appears to be a menu item name rather than a canonical food name; canonical name normalised to 'Dal Khichdi'. 3. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. Trans fat is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in a ghee-based preparation at this level per ICMR-NIN and USDA data. 6. Vitamin B12 is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a vegetarian dish with no animal-derived ingredients other than ghee, which contains negligible B12; confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN vegetarian dish data. 7. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate based on Atkinson et al. 2008 rice-lentil mixed dish entry from the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311069 is used for khichdi as an exact match. 9. FoodEx2 code A0CKJ (rice dish) is used as closest parent as no specific dal khichdi code exists in FoodEx2.
